name: Payment System
overview:
A payment system processes financial transactions between payers and payees, integrating with banks and
card networks. The system must guarantee exactly-once payment processing (no double charges), maintain
strong consistency for account balances, handle payment failures and retries safely, support multiple payment
methods (cards, bank transfers, wallets), and provide a complete audit trail. Correctness is paramount —
financial errors have legal and reputational consequences.

inScope: Support credit/debit cards, bank transfers, digital wallets

items: Support 10k transactions/second with exactly-once processing — no double charges under any failure
Payment API response latency <500ms; async processing for external provider calls
99.999% availability — payment downtime has direct financial and reputational impact
Strong consistency for ledger entries; ACID transactions required for all balance changes
Idempotency guaranteed for 24 hours per payment_id
Complete audit trail: every transaction immutably recorded for regulatory compliance

entities.name: Payment
entities.schema:
{
  payment_id: UUID,  // idempotency key
  payer_id: string,
  payee_id: string,
  amount: decimal,
  currency: string,
  method: enum(CARD|BANK_TRANSFER|WALLET),
  status: enum(PENDING|PROCESSING|COMPLETED|FAILED|REFUNDED),
  created_at: timestamp,
  completed_at: timestamp,

  external_ref: string  // bank/card network reference
}

entities.name: Account
entities.schema:
{
  account_id: string,
  user_id: string,
  balance: decimal,
  currency: string,
  version: int  // optimistic locking
}

endpoints.request: { payment_id (idempotency key), payer_id, payee_id, amount, currency, method }
endpoints.response:
{ payment_id, status: 'PENDING' }
Response 200: { payment_id, status }  // if idempotency key already used

approaches.title: No Idempotency
approaches.description: Process each API request as a new payment. Network retries cause double charges. A client that retries after a timeout may charge the user twice.
approaches.tradeoff: Double charges on retry; catastrophic for users.

approaches.title: Database Unique Constraint on payment_id
approaches.description: Store payment_id in a DB with a UNIQUE constraint. Duplicate requests fail with a unique violation. Correct for preventing duplicates but doesn't return the original response for duplicate requests. Client can't distinguish 'new payment' from 'duplicate payment'.
approaches.tradeoff: Doesn't return original response for duplicates; poor client experience.

approaches.title: Idempotency Key with Response Cache
approaches.description: Store payment_id → response in Redis with 24-hour TTL. On duplicate request, return cached response. The payment processing pipeline uses a distributed lock (Redis SETNX on payment_id) to prevent concurrent processing of the same payment. The lock is released after the payment is committed to the DB. Subsequent requests find the cached response.
approaches.tradeoff: Redis TTL means idempotency only guaranteed for 24 hours; Redis failure requires fallback.

approaches.title: Direct Balance Update
approaches.description: UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - amount WHERE account_id = payer_id. Race conditions: two concurrent payments can both read the same balance and both succeed, overdrawing the account.
approaches.tradeoff: Race conditions cause overdrafts; not safe for concurrent payments.

approaches.title: Optimistic Locking
approaches.description: Read balance + version, compute new balance, UPDATE WHERE version = old_version. Retry if version mismatch. Correct but high retry rate under contention. For accounts with many concurrent payments (e.g., a merchant account), retries can cascade.
approaches.tradeoff: High retry rate for hot accounts; retry storms possible.

approaches.title: Double-Entry Bookkeeping with Ledger
approaches.description: Use double-entry bookkeeping: every payment creates two ledger entries (debit payer, credit payee). Account balance = sum of all ledger entries. Ledger entries are immutable (append-only). No balance updates — only inserts. This eliminates race conditions on balance updates. Balance queries sum ledger entries (cached in Redis). Reconciliation jobs verify balance consistency.
approaches.tradeoff: Balance queries require summing ledger entries (mitigated by caching); ledger grows

components.name: Payment API
components.responsibility: Validates requests; checks idempotency cache; publishes to Kafka; returns status

components.name: Payment Processor
components.responsibility: Consumes Kafka; calls external payment provider (Stripe/Adyen); updates ledger

components.name: Ledger DB (PostgreSQL)
components.responsibility: Append-only double-entry ledger; immutable entries; ACID transactions

components.name: Idempotency Cache
components.responsibility: (Redis) Stores payment_id → response; 24-hour TTL; prevents duplicate processing

components.name: Provider
components.responsibility: Stripe/Adyen for card processing; bank APIs for transfers

components.name: Webhook Service
components.responsibility: Sends payment status updates to merchant webhooks; retries on failure

components.name: Reconciliation Service
components.responsibility: Daily reconciliation of ledger vs. external provider statements

components.name: Fraud Detection
components.responsibility: Rule-based + ML fraud scoring; blocks suspicious payments

questions.title: 2. Handling External Provider Failures
questions.content:
External payment providers (Stripe, Adyen) can fail or return ambiguous responses (timeout without
confirmation). The Payment Processor uses an idempotency key when calling the provider. On timeout, it
retries with the same idempotency key — the provider returns the original result if the payment was already
processed. If the provider is unavailable, the payment is queued for retry with exponential backoff.

questions.title: 3. Double-Entry Ledger
questions.content:
Every payment creates two ledger entries: DEBIT (payer account, amount) and CREDIT (payee account,
amount). The ledger is append-only — entries are never updated or deleted. Account balance = SUM(credits) -
SUM(debits). This is cached in Redis and updated atomically when new entries are added. The immutable
ledger provides a complete audit trail and makes reconciliation straightforward.

questions.title: 4. Reconciliation
questions.content:
Daily reconciliation compares the internal ledger against external provider statements. Discrepancies
(payments in the ledger but not in the provider statement, or vice versa) are flagged for manual review.
Common causes: network timeouts where the provider processed the payment but the response was lost, or
provider errors that weren't properly handled. Reconciliation is critical for financial accuracy.

questions.title: 5. PCI DSS Compliance
questions.content:
Card data (PAN, CVV) must never be stored in the payment system. The Payment API tokenizes card data
using the payment provider's tokenization service before any processing. Only the token is stored. The system
is deployed in a PCI DSS-compliant environment with network segmentation, encryption at rest and in transit,
and comprehensive audit logging.

keyFlows: ① Client sends payment → Payment API → check Idempotency Cache (Redis)
② New payment → Kafka → Payment Processor (saga: debit → charge → credit)
③ Payment Processor calls External Provider (Stripe) with idempotency key
④ Success → write double-entry to Ledger DB → notify via Webhook Svc
⑤ Daily Reconciliation Svc compares Ledger vs. Provider statements

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study the Saga pattern for distributed transactions: the difference between
choreography-based and orchestration-based sagas, how compensating transactions work, and how to
handle partial failures.
Expert Note: Learn about double-entry bookkeeping: the accounting equation (Assets = Liabilities +
Equity), how debits and credits work, and why it's the foundation of all financial systems.
Expert Note: Understand idempotency in payment systems: how idempotency keys prevent double
charges, the difference between idempotency and exactly-once delivery, and how to handle idempotency
key expiration.
Expert Note: Study PCI DSS requirements: the 12 requirements for cardholder data security, the difference
between SAQ levels, and how tokenization reduces PCI scope.
Expert Note: Learn about payment network protocols: how Visa/Mastercard authorization and settlement
work, the role of acquiring banks and issuing banks, and how chargebacks are processed.
